answer:
This is a weekly AI-news roundup (recorded Thursday July 9, published Friday) covering 18 chapters:

1) INTRO [00:08-00:12]: "a huge week in the world of AI," straight into the breakdown.

2) GPT-5.6 [00:15-03:24]: OpenAI's new flagship model is out; the host had brief early access [00:25-00:34]. It feels like a "monster leap" over 5.5 and the big story is "almost Fable-level intelligence, but for way cheaper" [01:17-01:25]. On-screen, the OpenAI announcement page reads "GPT-5.6: Frontier intelligence that scales with your ambition," dated July 9, 2026, tagline "More intelligence from every token, stronger performance per dollar, and more capability on demand for your hardest work," with sections including "Efficient by default, maximum performance on demand," "A leap forward in design," and "Availability and pricing" [00:36]. The page's demo tabs show Sailing game, Tiny code game, Museum website, Clockwork village game, and Interior design presentation, with copy saying GPT-5.6's frontend capabilities turn natural-language requests into polished interactive visualizations in ChatGPT Work [01:48, 01:38-01:58]. Benchmarks: on GDPval, 5.6 Soul beats everything except Claude Fable (and not by much) [02:10-02:27]; it beats everything including Mythos and Fable at browser computer-use, and ties Mythos on GPQA [02:29-02:40]. On BuseyBench it takes the top four spots (Soul Pro #1, Terra Pro #2, Terra #3, Soul #4; Luna models not in the top 10) [02:46-03:04]; the #1 generation cost 77 cents, 45,000 tokens, about a minute, vs GPT-5.5 Pro's 6 minutes and $4 [03:09-03:24].

3) OPENAI SUPER APP / CHATGPT WORK [03:38-12:52]: OpenAI announced "ChatGPT Work," described as the long-rumored super app: the Codex app, ChatGPT app, and Atlas browser are merged into one "ChatGPT app" [03:38-03:55]. On-screen: "ChatGPT is now a partner for your most ambitious work... ChatGPT Work is an agent that can take action across your apps and files, stay with a project for hours if needed, and turn a goal into finished work," with a "Download the ChatGPT desktop app" button [03:46]. It has Codex (coding) and Work (personal-assistant) modes [04:05-04:46], a built-in browser replacing Atlas [05:13-05:18], the 5.6 Soul/Terra/Luna models plus 5.5 and a simple slider from Terra Light up to Soul Ultra [05:38-05:55], and a "sites" feature that builds and hosts websites with no databases/CDN setup [05:58-06:12]. Demos: a Megabonk-clone 3D game ("Soulbon") built from one prompt in 31 minutes [06:26-06:32], shown running in the in-app browser [06:50 frame], then published on chatgpt.site and shared to "anyone on the internet" [07:32-07:56, share dialog visible at 07:56]; an "impress me" website built in 35 minutes on Soul Ultra [08:12-08:17]; a new side-chat sidebar [09:26-09:42]; and Work mode as a personal assistant connected to Gmail, Google Drive, Slack, and Granola [09:52-09:57] - the on-screen report shows it reviewed the last 30 days of Gmail, calendar May 8-Aug 8, Slack DMs/mentions/threads, 22 Granola meeting notes (May 12-Jul 7), 37 Drive documents, his second brain, and local repos [10:13 frame, 10:15-10:33]. It proposed a "daily control tower" and recurring scheduled tasks [10:50-11:29], visualized his Obsidian second brain (212 saved sources, 426 wiki pages, 28 journal entries, 14 memory pages) [11:39-11:45], and built a control-tower dashboard with today/AI-intel/brand-awareness tabs [12:16-12:52].

4) HYPERAGENT (sponsor) [12:55-16:09]: from the company behind Airtable, lets you build a team of AI agents, with a new community skills marketplace [12:55-13:12; "Install a skill from the community" with browse-catalog and one-click-install options on-screen at 13:04]. Demo of the host's B-roll generator skill: it plans shots from a script, routes prompts to Veo (realistic) or HyperFrames (motion graphics), and stitches clips with FFmpeg [13:15-14:33]; the on-screen shot list shows "The cut (60.0s, 1920x1080, 30fps, silent)" with 9 shots for a "Codex Micro" teaser [14:54 frame], and a generated list card "ONE BUTTON FOR: Start a task / Steer it / Approve changes / Review diffs" [15:18 frame].

5) GPT-LIVE [16:14-18:41]: OpenAI's new voice mode; you can interrupt it and it can interrupt you for natural conversation [16:21-16:27], plus real-time in-room translation (English-Hindi example) [16:33-16:54]. On-screen: "Introducing GPT-Live: a new generation of voice models for natural human-AI interaction, now powering ChatGPT Voice" [16:22]. Enable via settings > voice > model = live, with intelligence/speed options [17:08-17:24]; live demo where it stayed silent until told to respond and then recapped his episode plan [17:26-18:38].

6) GROK 4.5 [18:45-23:25]: xAI's surprise release; on-screen "Introducing Grok 4.5... smartest model built for coding, agentic tasks, and knowledge work" [18:54]. Competitive on DeepSWE and Terminal Bench [19:02-19:52], though a day later 5.6's numbers took the lead (DeepSWE: Grok 62, Fable 66, 5.6 72.7; Terminal Bench: Fable 84, Grok 83, 5.6 88 / Soul Ultra 91.9) [19:57-20:19]. Announcement page has a solar-system animation, slide decks, and a free CLI install command [20:31-21:05; page note visible at 20:50 that Grok 4.5 is not yet available in the EU, expected mid-July]. On BuseyBench it lands ~#21 [21:36; leaderboard cards for GPT-5.6 Luna/Sol variants visible at 21:13]. Its "impress me" site was a star/constellation observatory ("Every star is a memory of light") [21:45-22:58, visible at 21:55]. API pricing: $2/M input, $6/M output vs 5.6 Soul $5/$30, Terra $2.50/$15, Luna $1/$6 [23:05-23:20].

7) MUSE SPARK 1.1 (Meta) [23:28-26:14]: a surprisingly decent model, on par with last-gen frontier models: Terminal Bench 80 vs Opus 4.8's 82.7 and GPT-5.5's 83.4; DeepSWE 53.3 vs 59 and 67 [23:41-23:59]. The on-screen Meta "Evaluations" table compares Muse Spark 1.1 against Muse Spark, Gemini 3.1 Pro (high), Opus 4.8 (max), and GPT 5.5 (xhigh) across agent/coding/multimodal rows including Terminal-Bench 2.1, SWE-Bench Pro, and DeepSWE 1.1 [23:37]. Pricing $1.25/M input, $4.25/M output [24:21-24:26]; $20 free credits; its BuseyBench run took 10,269 tokens and 58 seconds [24:44-24:55], landing ~#36 [25:20]; huge visual leap from April-2025 Llama Scout [25:04-25:13, timeline frame at 24:58]. Its "impress me" site ("Golden Hour") was judged better than Grok's [25:52-26:06, visible at 25:57]. BuseyBench leaderboard frame shows #2 GPT-5.6 Terra Pro (6.8), #3 GPT-5.6 Terra (6.7), #4 GPT-5.6 Sol (6.4), #5 Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview, #6 GPT-5.5 Pro [25:34].

8) FABLE COMPARISON [26:18-27:31]: Claude Fable (not new this week) still won the "impress me" test with a "small museum of computational art" featuring interactive pieces: the field, the swarm (boids that stay close/align/don't collide), Rorschach-style ink, garden of life, and a musical "resonance" piece [26:25-27:31].

9) MUSE IMAGE (Meta) [27:40-29:01]: new, controversial image model: it connects to Instagram and can generate images of anybody you @mention, which many people dislike [27:42-27:59]. Free inside Meta AI [27:59-28:04]; he generated images of himself, a friend, and Mr. Beast without needing consent from the latter [28:04-28:47]. There's an opt-out setting, but reportedly not everyone has it yet [28:47-29:01]. On-screen: Meta newsroom "Introducing Muse Image: Image Generation Built for Your World" [27:49].

10) NEW FUTURE TOOLS [29:05-29:10]: tongue-in-cheek "biggest news of the week": the host launched his redesigned Future Tools website.

11) MORE FABLE ACCESS [29:28-30:16]: Claude Fable 5 access was extended from July 7 to July 12 (on-screen X post from Claude: "We're extending access to Claude Fable 5 on all paid plans through July 12" [29:37]); after user complaints (including his own) about exhausted weekly limits, Anthropic reset weekly limits on Thursday July 9, the day GPT-5.6 came out [29:43-30:16].

12) CLAUDE COWORK MOBILE [30:26-31:09]: Claude Cowork is coming to mobile and web; work now runs in the cloud so it continues when your laptop closes, and you can check it from your phone (mirroring the new ChatGPT app's cloud execution) [30:26-31:09]. On-screen Anthropic blog: "Claude Cowork is coming to mobile and web. Your work goes everywhere with you, and keeps going without you," dated July 7, 2026, with beta access rolling out over the next several weeks starting with Max users [30:34].

13) CLAUDE REFLECT [31:11-31:37]: a "Spotify wrapped"-style recap of your Claude usage via a new reflect button in settings: most active day, peak hour, conversation graph, top topics; on Claude web and desktop [31:13-31:37]. On-screen page: "See your patterns and shape them," with look-back over 1, 3, 6, or 12 months [31:22].

14) J-SPACE [31:38-32:44]: Anthropic research into what he describes as an AI's "subconscious": patterns held in the back of the model's mind beyond its visible chain of thought, which Anthropic is starting to track [31:42-32:34]. Illustrated on-screen by ASCII art of a boat made of the word BOAT on an ocean of the word OCEAN [31:47].

15) GOOGLE PHOTOS VIDEO UPDATE [32:45-33:01]: Video Remix in Google Photos creates shareable stylized video clips using Gemini Omni; available on Google AI Plus, Pro, and Ultra plans [32:45-33:01]. On-screen Google blog: "Create shareable video clips in seconds with Video Remix in Google Photos," Jul 08, 2026 [32:54].

16) SEEDREAM 5.0 PRO (ByteDance) [33:03-33:55]: new image model strong at dense infographics, interactive editing (circle/draw instructions), Photoshop-like layer separation, and photorealism [33:05-33:39]; playable in Leonardo [33:42]; he had it make a J-Space infographic and a Gary Busey image (Busey render visible on his monitor at 34:12).

17) GPT-5.6 VS FABLE [34:05-36:42]: closing comparison: both feel like the biggest leap since GPT-3.5 to GPT-4 [34:13-34:26]; 3-hour autonomous builds come back ~98% done [34:44-35:04]; Fable is a bit smarter architecturally, 5.6 is better at "dump a huge brief and walk away" [35:14-35:45]; 5.6 (Soul and Luna) will be his daily driver with Fable as second opinion [35:49-36:09]; GPT-5.6 found security vulnerabilities (including API-key exposure) that Fable missed in his Fable-built Future Tools site [36:11-36:38]. Plus channel notes: Thursday recording cadence, midweek tutorials [36:58-38:15].

18) WTF? [38:16-38:40]: gag outro: a stream of water pours onto his empty studio chair (visible at the 38:26 frame) with the line "What's everyone doing? WELCOME TO THE SHOW" [38:40].

WHAT'S ACTUALLY NEW (hype stripped out):

Release: GPT-5.6 is OpenAI's new flagship model, out of preview the day of recording, Thursday July 9 [00:15][18:38][30:08]; the on-screen OpenAI announcement page is dated July 9, 2026 and is subtitled 'More intelligence from every token, stronger performance per dollar, and more capability on demand' [00:22 frame]. It ships as a family of tiers: Soul (most powerful), Terra, Luna [02:20][23:14-23:20], plus Pro variants (Soul Pro, Terra Pro) [02:51-02:57] and a Soul Ultra setting [08:12][20:19].

Capabilities (concrete):
- GDPval (economically valuable tasks): 5.6 Soul outperforms everything except Claude Fable, and Fable 'doesn't outperform it by much' [02:10-02:27]. No numeric scores shown or spoken.
- Computer/browser use: beats everything tested, including Mythos and Fable [02:29-02:33].
- GPQA: ties Mythos [02:35-02:40].
- Deep SWE bench: 72.7% vs Fable 66 and Grok 4.5 62, making it the new leader [19:57-20:02].
- Terminal bench: 88, with Soul Ultra at 91.9%, vs Fable 84 and Grok 83 [20:07-20:19].
- SVG 'Buybench': 5.6 takes the top four spots (Soul Pro #1, Terra Pro #2, Terra #3, Soul #4); no Luna model in the top 10 [02:46-03:03].
- Practical: one-prompt built a playable 3D shooter in 31 minutes [06:26-06:32, frame t06:33 shows it served on localhost:5173]; a 35-minute Soul Ultra run built an interactive site ('ATLAS/NULL... raining upwards') [08:12-08:21, frame t08:08]; it audited code Fable wrote for the Future Tools site and caught security vulnerabilities Fable missed, including a potential API-key exposure [36:11-36:33].

Speed (concrete): On the same Buybench generation, 5.6 Soul Pro took about 1 minute and 45,000 tokens where GPT-5.5 Pro took 6 minutes, roughly 6x faster generation on that task [03:09-03:24].

Cost (spoken; no pricing table shown on screen):
- Same Buybench generation: $0.77 on 5.6 Soul Pro vs $4 on GPT-5.5 Pro, about 5x cheaper [03:09-03:24].
- API pricing: Soul $5/M input, $30/M output; Terra $2.50/M input, $15/M output; Luna $1/M input, $6/M output (given as comparison to Grok 4.5's $2/$6) [23:05-23:20].
- The stated value proposition: 'almost as good as Fable-level intelligence, but for way cheaper' [01:20-01:25].

CONFLICT RECONCILED: At [24:26-24:33] the presenter calls Terra 'the lowest end 5.6 model' when comparing it to Muse Spark's $1.25/$4.25 pricing. His own pricing list at [23:14-23:20] contradicts this: Luna ($1/$6) is cheaper than Terra ($2.50/$15), and Muse Spark's pricing actually sits nearest Luna, as he himself notes at [23:20-23:27] ('pretty close to the Luna model in pricing'). The primary evidence supports Luna as the cheapest tier; the 'Terra is lowest end' remark is a likely misstatement.

HYPE EXCLUDED: 'feels like it should have been GPT-6', 'monster leap' [00:55-01:00], 'huge leap over 5.5' [00:41-00:48], the GPT-3.5-to-4 comparison [34:13-34:26], 'blowing my mind' [35:04-35:08], 'most fun I've had in a year and a half' / 'the worst it's ever going to be' [36:42-36:54] are impressions, not measurements. One subjective but usage-grounded take, labeled as opinion: Fable feels slightly smarter for architectural decisions, while 5.6 is better at long unattended runs from a big brief and gets closer to intent [35:10-35:49].

EVIDENCE GAPS: No on-screen pricing or benchmark table for GPT-5.6 appears in the frames (the announcement frame at [00:22] shows an 'Availability and pricing' sidebar section but no legible numbers; the only numeric table frame, t23:37, is Meta's Muse Spark eval table with a GPT-5.5 column, not 5.6). All 5.6 pricing and benchmark figures are spoken only. GDPval/GPQA/computer-use are relative claims with no numbers. Nothing in the evidence on context window, knowledge cutoff, rate limits, subscription-plan pricing, or which tiers the presenter's demos used beyond Soul Pro / Soul Ultra.

WHAT IT IS: The "super app" is OpenAI's newly announced ChatGPT Work / new ChatGPT app. Per the host, "this is essentially the super app that they've been talking about": OpenAI is discontinuing the separate Codex app, ChatGPT app, and Atlas browser and merging all three into one app just called the ChatGPT app [03:38-03:55]. The on-screen OpenAI announcement (dated July 9, 2026) reads: "ChatGPT is now a partner for your most ambitious work. ChatGPT Work is an agent that can take action across your apps and files, stay with a project for hours if needed, and turn a goal into finished work," with a "Download the ChatGPT desktop app" button [03:46 frame].

WHAT IT DOES:

1) Two modes via a dropdown - Codex and Work [04:05-04:07]. Codex mode is for writing code/development (it shows branches and where on your computer to develop) [04:19-04:55]; Work mode is a personal-assistant mode for "getting stuff done": you state what you want and it decides whether to answer, connect to a tool, or go build something, similar to Claude Cowork [04:26-04:48]. The host's one complaint: the UI barely changes between modes, so the difference isn't obvious [04:10-04:17]. Work mode asks which plugins to connect and "can connect to a lot of stuff" [05:02-05:04].

2) Built-in browser (Atlas replacement) and files: in Work mode the right sidebar shows browser and files; Atlas is being wrapped up and the browser now lives inside ChatGPT [05:05-05:18]. In Codex mode the sidebar adds coding tools: review and a terminal, plus the same browser/files [05:20-05:32].

3) New models with a simplicity slider: access to GPT-5.6 Soul, Terra, and Luna models plus 5.5; with advanced mode off, a slider runs from 5.6 Terra Light (smallest) up to 5.6 Soul Ultra (best, burns more credits) [05:38-05:55].

4) Sites - prompt-to-hosted-website: a sites button lets you prompt websites into existence; ChatGPT handles hosting, databases, and CDNs [05:58-06:12]. Demo: his one-prompt 3D "Soulbon" game (built in 31 minutes [06:26-06:32], shown running in the in-app browser at the 06:50 frame) was moved onto sites, fully hosted at chatgpt.site [07:26-07:34], then shared: a share dialog switches access from "Just me" to "Anyone on the internet" and publishes a public URL (dialog visible at 07:56) [07:46-07:56]. A second demo: a 35-minute Soul Ultra build of an interactive showcase website [08:12-08:17].

5) Side-chat panel: a chat button opens a sidebar so you can pick a model and ask quick questions without leaving the project in the main window [09:26-09:42].

6) Personal assistant across your apps (Work mode): it connects to Gmail, Google Drive, Slack, Granola, "anything you use" [09:52-09:57]. In his test it reviewed: last 30 days of Gmail inbox, calendar May 8-August 8, recent Slack DMs/mentions/threads, 22 Granola meeting notes (May 12-July 7), 37 recently viewed/modified Google Drive documents, his second brain/journals, and recent activity in local projects and repositories [10:15-10:33; the on-screen "Find productivity opportunities" report at 10:13 also shows an analysis section ("What is actually costing you time... roughly 3.5 scheduled hours per active weekday... the bigger problem is schedule volatility") and output files "Daily Control Tower.md" and "Matt Wolfe Operating System.md"]. It then produced a priority list and a "what I should own for you" agent offer: build a daily control tower (what needs him today, what can wait, what's blocked on others, deadline collisions, one creative priority, items it can finish alone) [10:40-11:01], extract decisions after every Granola meeting, cross-check them against Slack/Drive/calendar, draft missing documents and flag promises [11:05-11:10], and help with sponsorships and editorial content [11:13-11:22].

7) Scheduled recurring tasks: any of those jobs can be made daily, weekly, or monthly via the scheduled section [11:22-11:29].

8) Second-brain visualization: asked to visualize his Obsidian vault, it found 212 saved sources, 426 wiki pages, 28 journal entries, and 14 memory pages, and generated a visualizer of where his attention goes [11:29-11:56].

9) Control-tower dashboard: it extended his existing audience dashboard with a today tab (watching email and Slack for what needs attention now), an AI-intel news-alert page, a brand-awareness page like Google Alerts watching for his name and the Future Tools brand, and website data; now his default browser window [12:16-12:52]. The on-screen brand page at 12:50 is titled "Mentions that are actually yours," showing scanning of Google News for his name/brands with mention counts and cards for Future Tools, Matt Wolfe, BuseyBench, and MattWolfe.com.

10) Cloud execution: tasks in the new ChatGPT app run in the cloud rather than locally, so work continues when your computer sleeps and can be managed from your phone [30:34-30:41, 31:05-31:09] - a capability Anthropic matched the same week with Claude Cowork on mobile/web (Anthropic blog visible at 30:34).

11) Voice (GPT-Live): the same week OpenAI launched GPT-Live, the new voice mode in the app - natural two-way interruption and real-time in-room translation (e.g., English-Hindi) [16:14-16:54]; on-screen: "Introducing GPT-Live: a new generation of voice models... now powering ChatGPT Voice" [16:22]. It's enabled in the app under settings > voice > model = live, with intelligence-vs-speed options [17:08-17:24].

EVIDENCE LIMITS: the evidence doesn't state pricing/plan requirements for the new app, mobile-platform specifics, or an exhaustive plugin list; and the "super app" framing is the host's interpretation ("from everything I can tell and everything I've been told") of the ChatGPT Work announcement [03:38-03:42].
